title: Sysctl Parameter Changes Detection
spl:
index=os sourcetype=linux_audit action=modified path=/proc/sys/*
| stats count by host, path, exe, auid
| where count > 0
description: Identifies modifications to kernel parameters that affect system behavior, security posture, or performance tuning.
value: Identifies modifications to kernel parameters that affect system behavior, security posture, or performance tuning.
implementation: Set up auditctl rules to monitor changes to /proc/sys and /etc/sysctl.conf. Create alerts for unexpected sysctl modifications, especially those affecting network (ip_forward, tcp_syncookies) or IPC parameters.

references.title: Splunk Lantern ŌĆö use case library

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
ŌĆó Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`.
ŌĆó Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=linux_audit, /proc/sys monitoring`.
ŌĆó For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ŌĆö Configure data collection
Set up auditctl rules to monitor changes to /proc/sys and /etc/sysctl.conf. Create alerts for unexpected sysctl modifications, especially those affecting network (ip_forward, tcp_syncookies) or IPC parameters.

Step 2 ŌĆö Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=linux_audit action=modified path=/proc/sys/*
| stats count by host, path, exe, auid
| where count > 0
```

Understanding this SPL

**Sysctl Parameter Changes Detection** ŌĆö Identifies modifications to kernel parameters that affect system behavior, security posture, or performance tuning.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=linux_audit, /proc/sys monitoring`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedŌĆörename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: linux_audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

ŌĆó Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=linux_audit. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
ŌĆó `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, path, exe, auid** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
ŌĆó Filters the current rows with `where count > 0` ŌĆö typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 ŌĆö Validate
On the host, compare with `top`, `htop`, `vmstat`, `iostat`, or `sar` as appropriate to this use case. For log-only detections, compare with the relevant file under `/var/log` (or `journalctl`) on a test host. Confirm that indexed event counts and field values line up with what you see on the system and that your role can search the right indexes and fields.

Step 4 ŌĆö Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Timeline
grandmaExplanation: We spot modifications to kernel parameters that affect system behavior, security posture, or performance tuning ŌĆö so you find out before users do when something is slowing down or breaking.
knownFalsePositives: Hardening, containers, and orchestration can change `sysctl` often. Distinguish policy drift from expected automation using host class and change tickets.
